Das gesetzliche Rentensystem basiert auf:
The statutory pension system is based on **earnings points**:

- 1 point = paying in for 1 year at the average wage (~€45,000 gross)
- With 45 working years: ~45 points
- Pension value 2024: ~€37.60 per point
- 45 points × €37.60 = **~€1,692 monthly pension**

⚠️ That is the maximum for average earners. Low-income earners receive less.
Dass Einkommen nicht zweimal versteuert: That income is not taxed twice when working internationally
Dein **Bruttolohn** ist was im Vertrag s:

Your **net salary** is what lands in your bank account.

Deductions:
- **Income tax** (~14–45%)
- **Social contributions**: pension, health, long-term care, unemployment insurance (~21%)

With €3,000 gross, only ~€1,900–2,100 net often ends up in your account.

Der **Zinseszinseffekt**: Du bekommst Zi:
The **compound interest effect**: You earn interest on your capital AND on the interest you have already received.

Example: €1,000 at 7% per year:
- After 10 years: €1,967
- After 20 years: €3,870
- After 30 years: €7,612

The secret: **Time**. The earlier you start, the more powerful the effect.
Der **effektive Jahreszins** (EJZ) zeigt:
The **annual percentage rate** (APR) shows the true total cost of a loan – including all fees.

Example: €5,000 loan at 8% APR over 5 years:
- Monthly payment: ~€101
- Total repayment: ~€6,060
- You pay €1,060 extra!

Always compare the **APR**, never the nominal interest rate.
Der Anteil des Kredits den du jährlich z: The share of the loan you repay annually
Der Börsenwert des Vorstands: The market value of the management board
Der Jahresumsatz eines Unternehmens: The annual revenue of a company

Der Zinseszins ist eine zweischneidige W:
Compound interest is a double-edged sword. With **debt**, it works against you:

**Overdraft** at 12% p.a.: Someone who leaves €1,000 in overdraft and pays nothing owes after 5 years: **1,000 × (1.12)⁵ = €1,762**.

**Credit card debt** in the US: often 20–25% interest. That doubles the debt in ~3.5 years!

Strategy: Debts with high interest always have the highest priority. Every euro of debt reduction guaranteed delivers the interest rate as a "return".

Die **4%-Regel** (Trinity Study, 1998):\:
The **4% rule** (Trinity Study, 1998):

If you withdraw a maximum of 4% of your portfolio annually, it has historically lasted 30+ years.

**FIRE number** = Annual expenses × 25

Example: €2,000/month = €24,000/year → FIRE number: **€600,000**
Die **4%-Regel** stammt aus der Trinity:
The **4% rule** comes from the Trinity Study (1994): You can withdraw 4% of the portfolio annually and it lasts 30 years.

**Criticism:**
⚠️ Based on US data 1926–1994
⚠️ 30 years – someone who retires at 35 needs 50+ years
⚠️ Dangerous with low returns

**Alternative rules of thumb:**
- 3% for very early retirement (ERE)
- 3.5% as a compromise
- Flexible withdrawals (less during crises)
Die **50/30/20-Regel**:\n\n- **50%** für:
The **50/30/20 rule**:

- **50%** for basic needs (rent, groceries, transport)
- **30%** for wants & leisure
- **20%** for saving & debt repayment

A simple budget book – whether an app or Excel – shows you where your money really goes.
Die **72er-Regel** ist eine Faustregel f:
The **rule of 72** is a rule of thumb for quick back-of-the-envelope calculations:

**Doubling time (years) = 72 ÷ interest rate (%)**

Examples:
- At **2% interest** (savings account): 72 ÷ 2 = **36 years** to double
- At **7% interest** (ETF): 72 ÷ 7 ≈ **10.3 years** to double
- At **12% interest**: 72 ÷ 12 = **6 years**

The rule also works in reverse: if you know in how many years you want your money to double, you know what interest rate you need.
Die **Rentenlücke** ist die Differenz zw:
The **pension gap** is the difference between your last salary and your later pension.

Example: Gross salary €3,500 → statutory pension approx. €1,600 gross. Net perhaps €1,300 remains.

But: In old age you still pay health insurance, possibly taxes – and the cost of living rises due to inflation.

Rule of thumb: **At least 80% of the last net salary** is needed to maintain a similar standard of living. The gap must be closed privately.
Die **Riester-Rente** bietet staatliche:
The **Riester pension** offers state subsidies:
- **€175 basic allowance** per year
- **€300 child allowance** per child (born from 2008)
- Tax deductibility up to €2,100/year

Who benefits from it?
✅ Families with children (high child allowances)
✅ Low-income earners (state allowance makes up a large share)

Who benefits less?
❌ Childless individuals with medium/high income
❌ Those who need high flexibility (capital is locked in)
Die **Rürup-Rente** (auch Basisrente) is:
The **Rürup pension** (also called basic pension) is specifically designed for the self-employed and freelancers who do not pay into the statutory pension.

Advantage: Contributions up to **€27,566 (2024)** can be deducted as special expenses for tax purposes – the higher the income, the more you save.

Disadvantages: The capital is **not inheritable**, cannot be used as collateral, cannot be cancelled. You always receive a lifelong pension – no lump sum.

Conclusion: Worthwhile for high earners as tax optimization – but less flexible than an ETF portfolio.

**Improving your score**: Pay bills on time, avoid unnecessary loans. Request a **free self-disclosure** once a year (Art. 15 GDPR).
Die **TER** (Total Expense Ratio) ist di:
The **TER** (Total Expense Ratio) is the annual total cost ratio of an ETF. It is automatically deducted daily – you pay it without noticing.

Example: €50,000 at 7% return over 30 years:
- With **0.2% TER**: Final value ~**€366,000**
- With **1.5% TER**: Final value ~**€266,000**
→ Difference: **€100,000** – just from costs!

Further costs:
- **Order fees** when buying (broker-dependent, €0–1 at neo-brokers)
- **Spread** (difference between buying and selling price)
- **Custody fees** (usually €0 at neo-brokers)
Die **reale Rendite** ist das, was nach:
The **real return** is what remains after deducting inflation:

**Real return = Nominal return − Inflation**

Example:
- Savings account: 1% interest − 3% inflation = **−2% real** → loss of purchasing power!
- Overnight money: 3.5% − 3% = **+0.5% real** → barely any gain
- MSCI World ETF: 7% − 3% = **+4% real** → genuine wealth accumulation

Conclusion: Anyone who leaves their money in a savings account loses real purchasing power through inflation – even if the number in the account rises.

Die Zinseszins-Formel lautet:\\n\\n**K_n:
The compound interest formula is:

**K_n = K_0 × (1 + p)ⁿ**

Where:
- **K_0** = starting capital
- **p** = interest rate per period (e.g. 0.07 for 7%)
- **n** = number of periods (years)
- **K_n** = final capital

Example: €5,000 at 7% for 20 years:
5,000 × (1.07)²⁰ = 5,000 × 3.87 = **€19,348**

You have almost quadrupled your capital – without paying in a single cent more!

Die gesetzliche Rentenversicherung funkt:
The statutory pension insurance operates according to the **pay-as-you-go system**: Your contributions today flow directly to today's pensioners – you are not saving up your own capital.

Current contribution rate: **18.6% of gross salary** (split equally between employee and employer).

Problem: Fewer and fewer contributors must finance more and more pensioners. The **pension level** is falling from a former 53% to today's ~48% – with a continuing downward trend.

ETFs come in two variants:

**Accumulating**:
Dividends are automatically reinvested. Capital grows faster through the compound interest effect. Taxes are only due when selling (except the advance lump sum).

**Distributing**:
Dividends are paid out regularly – e.g. quarterly. An immediate "passive income stream" – but you must pay taxes immediately and reinvest yourself.

**Conclusion for wealth accumulation**: Accumulating is usually better – less tax friction, more compound interest effect.

**Conclusion for retirement/withdrawals**: Distributing is more practical for regular income.

**Simple interest** means: You receive interest every year only on your original capital.

Example: €1,000 at 5% → every year +€50 → after 10 years: €1,500.

**Compound interest** means: You receive interest on the interest already earned as well.

Example: €1,000 at 5% compound interest → after 10 years: €1,629. After 30 years: €4,322 instead of just €2,500!

The difference may seem small at first – over time it becomes gigantic.

With the **snowball method** you pay off the smallest debt first – regardless of the interest rate. The freed-up money you roll onto the next.

**Alternative – avalanche method**: Pay off the highest-interest debt first. Saves more interest, requires more discipline.

Das deutsche Rentensystem ruht auf **dre:
The German pension system rests on **three pillars**:

**Pillar 1 – Statutory pension**: Compulsory insurance for employees. Financed through current contributions (pay-as-you-go system).

**Pillar 2 – Company pension scheme (bAV)**: Your employer contributes. Tax-advantaged.

**Pillar 3 – Private provision**: Riester, Rürup, ETF portfolio. Voluntary, but indispensable.

The goal: Combine all three pillars to maintain your standard of living in old age.
